{{Shrine |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), haisho.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = Kyoto City, Kamigyo Ward, within Kyoto Gyoen 9 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|08.4|N|135|45|40.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}} '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命}}, and {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}} (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|ja|藤原冬嗣}} enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} in the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * {{ill|Kenchō|ja|建治}} 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} and Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} * {{Wayback|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=1701|title=Takiiribime Shrine|date=20041121152814}} * [https://ja.kyoto.travel/tourism/single02.php?Category:Pages [[ja:宗像神社 (京都市)]]

{{Shrine |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), haisho.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = Kyoto City, Kamigyo Ward, within Kyoto Gyoen 9 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|08.4|N|135|45|40.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}} '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命}}, and {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}} (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|ja|藤原冬嗣}} enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} in the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * {{ill|Kenchō|ja|建治}} 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} and Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} * {{Wayback|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=1701|title=Takiiribime Shrine|date=20041121152814}} * [https://ja.kyoto.travel/tourism/single02.php?Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Munakata Shrine (Kyoto)]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category_id=7&tourism_id=666 Munakata Shrine - Kyoto Travel Navi] [[Category:Munakata Shrine Worship Hall |location = Kyoto City, Kamigyo Ward, within Kyoto Gyoen 9 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|08.4|N|135|45|40.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}} '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命}}, and {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}} (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|ja|藤原冬嗣}} enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} in the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * {{ill|Kenchō|ja|建治}} 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} and Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} * {{Wayback|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=1701|title=Takiiribime Shrine|date=20041121152814}} * [https://ja.kyoto.travel/tourism/single02.php?Category:Pages {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), haisho.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = Kyoto City, Kamigyo Ward, within Kyoto Gyoen 9 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|08.4|N|135|45|40.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}} '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命}}, and {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}} (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|ja|藤原冬嗣}} enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} in the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * {{ill|Kenchō|ja|建治}} 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} and Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} * {{Wayback|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=1701|title=Takiiribime Shrine|date=20041121152814}} * [https://ja.kyoto.travel/tourism/single02.php?Category:Pages {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), haisho.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = Kyoto City, Kamigyo Ward, within Kyoto Gyoen 9 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|08.4|N|135|45|40.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}} '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命}}, and {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}} (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|ja|藤原冬嗣}} enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} in the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * {{ill|Kenchō|ja|建治}} 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} and Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} * {{Wayback|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=1701|title=Takiiribime Shrine|date=20041121152814}} * [https://ja.kyoto.travel/tourism/single02.php?Category:Pages {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}} '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}, and {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}} (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|ja|藤原冬嗣}} enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} in the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * {{ill|Kenchō|ja|建治}} 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} and Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}} '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}, and {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}} (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|ja|藤原冬嗣}} enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} in the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * {{ill|Kenchō|ja|建治}} 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} and Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), haisho.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = Kyoto City, Kamigyo Ward, within Kyoto Gyoen 9 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|08.4|N|135|45|40.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}} '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}, and {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}} (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|ja|藤原冬嗣}} enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} in the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * {{ill|Kenchō|ja|建治}} 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} and Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), haisho.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = Kyoto City, Kamigyo Ward, within Kyoto Gyoen 9 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|08.4|N|135|45|40.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}} '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}, and {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}} (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|ja|藤原冬嗣}} enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} in the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * {{ill|Kenchō|ja|建治}} 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} and Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} * {{Wayback|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=1701|title=Takiiribime Shrine|date=20041121152814}} * [https://ja.kyoto.travel/tourism/single02.php?Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Munakata Shrine (Kyoto)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Munakata Shrine (Kyoto)]][[Category:qq]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), haisho.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = Kyoto City, Kamigyo Ward, within Kyoto Gyoen 9 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|08.4|N|135|45|40.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}} '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}, and {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}} (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|ja|藤原冬嗣}} enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} in the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * {{ill|Kenchō|ja|建治}} 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} and Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} * {{Wayback|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=1701|title=Takiiribime Shrine|date=20041121152814}} * [https://ja.kyoto.travel/tourism/single02.php?Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Munakata Shrine (Kyoto)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Munakata Shrine (Kyoto)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Munakata Shrine (Kyoto)]][[Category:kk]] [[sn:Munakata Shrine (Kyoto)]][[Category:qq]] {{Munakata Faith}}[[ja:宗像神社 (京都市)]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), haisho.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = Kyoto City, Kamigyo Ward, within Kyoto Gyoen 9 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|08.4|N|135|45|40.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}} '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}, and {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}} (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|ja|藤原冬嗣}} enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} in the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * {{ill|Kenchō|ja|建治}} 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} and Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), haisho.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = Kyoto City, Kamigyo Ward, within Kyoto Gyoen 9 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|08.4|N|135|45|40.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}} '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}, and {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}} (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|ja|藤原冬嗣}} enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} in the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * {{ill|Kenchō|ja|建治}} 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} and Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} * {{Wayback|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=1701|title=Takiiribime Shrine|date=20041121152814}} * [https://ja.kyoto.travel/tourism/single02.php?Category:Pages {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), haisho.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = Kyoto City, Kamigyo Ward, within Kyoto Gyoen 9 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|08.4|N|135|45|40.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}} '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} and Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), haisho.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = Kyoto City, Kamigyo Ward, within Kyoto Gyoen 9 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|08.4|N|135|45|40.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}, and {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}} (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|ja|藤原冬嗣}} enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} in the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * {{ill|Kenchō|ja|建治}} 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} and Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} * {{Wayback|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=1701|title=Takiiribime Shrine|date=20041121152814}} * [https://ja.kyoto.travel/tourism/single02.php?Category:Pages revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), haisho.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = Kyoto City, Kamigyo Ward, within Kyoto Gyoen 9 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|08.4|N|135|45|40.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}, and {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}} (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|ja|藤原冬嗣}} enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} in the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * {{ill|Kenchō|ja|建治}} 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} and Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} * {{Wayback|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=1701|title=Takiiribime Shrine|date=20041121152814}} * [https://ja.kyoto.travel/tourism/single02.php?Category:Pages {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), haisho.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = Kyoto City, Kamigyo Ward, within Kyoto Gyoen 9 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|08.4|N|135|45|40.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}, and {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}} (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|ja|藤原冬嗣}} enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} in the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * {{ill|Kenchō|ja|建治}} 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} and Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), haisho.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = Kyoto City, Kamigyo Ward, within Kyoto Gyoen 9 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|08.4|N|135|45|40.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime}}, and {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸島比売命}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}} (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|ja|藤原冬嗣}} enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社}} in the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * {{ill|Kenchō|ja|建治}} 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} and Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} and Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja
