{{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shrine rank|WD=Q10444029}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shrine rank|WD=Q10444029}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiiribime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ|1=Takiribime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932870}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ}}, and {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神|1=Ukanomitama|2=ja|3=倉稲魂神|4=de|5=Ukanomitama|6=eo|7=Ukanomitama|8=mni|9=ꯎꯀꯅꯣꯃꯤꯇꯃ|10=ru|11=Ука-но_митама|12=simple|13=Ukanomitama|14=sv|15=Ukanomitama|16=zh|17=宇迦之御魂神|lt=Ukanomitama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. [[:en:Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu]] enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|1=Minamoto no Morofusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564069}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher Ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin’i|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神階|1=shinkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591025}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajuji family|1=Kazan'in family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615289}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]], the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan]] 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * [[:en:Kenji (era)|Kenchō]] 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushinadahime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Matsuri]]. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} and Emperor [[:en:Emperor Sutoku|Sutoku]]. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} * {{Wayback|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=1701|title=Takiiribime Shrine|date=20041121152814}} * [https://ja.kyoto.travel/tourism/single02.php?Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category_id=7&tourism_id=666 Munakata Shrine - Kyoto Travel Navi] {{Munakata Faith}} {{translated page|ja|宗像神社 (京都市)|version=94851838|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:宗像神社 (京都市)]] {{Commons category|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} [[zh:宗像神社 (京都市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11451877}} [[Category:Buildings in Kamigyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Kyoto]] [[Category:Fu-sha]] [[Category:Heian-kyō]] [[Category:History of Kamigyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Kazan'in family]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神|1=Ukanomitama|2=ja|3=倉稲魂神|4=de|5=Ukanomitama|6=eo|7=Ukanomitama|8=mni|9=ꯎꯀꯅꯣꯃꯤꯇꯃ|10=ru|11=Ука-но_митама|12=simple|13=Ukanomitama|14=sv|15=Ukanomitama|16=zh|17=宇迦之御魂神|lt=Ukanomitama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajuji family|1=Kazan'in family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615289}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]], the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan]] 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * [[:en:Kenji (era)|Kenchō]] 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushinadahime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ}}. {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shrine rank|WD=Q10444029}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shrine rank|WD=Q10444029}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiiribime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ|1=Takiribime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932870}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ}}, and {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神|1=Ukanomitama|2=ja|3=倉稲魂神|4=de|5=Ukanomitama|6=eo|7=Ukanomitama|8=mni|9=ꯎꯀꯅꯣꯃꯤꯇꯃ|10=ru|11=Ука-но_митама|12=simple|13=Ukanomitama|14=sv|15=Ukanomitama|16=zh|17=宇迦之御魂神|lt=Ukanomitama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of [[:en:Enryaku|Enryaku]] (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. [[:en:Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu]] enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|1=Minamoto no Morofusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564069}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher Ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin’i|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神階|1=shinkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591025}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajuji family|1=Kazan'in family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615289}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]], the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan]] 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * [[:en:Kenji (era)|Kenchō]] 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushinadahime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Matsuri]]. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} and Emperor [[:en:Emperor Sutoku|Sutoku]]. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} * {{Wayback|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=1701|title=Takiiribime Shrine|date=20041121152814}} * [https://ja.kyoto.travel/tourism/single02.php?Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category_id=7&tourism_id=666 Munakata Shrine - Kyoto Travel Navi] {{Munakata Faith}} {{translated page|ja|宗像神社 (京都市)|version=94851838|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:宗像神社 (京都市)]] {{Commons category|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} [[zh:宗像神社 (京都市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11451877}} [[Category:Buildings in Kamigyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Kyoto]] [[Category:Fu-sha]] [[Category:Heian-kyō]] [[Category:History of Kamigyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Kazan'in family]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

{{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shrine rank|WD=Q10444029}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shrine rank|WD=Q10444029}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiiribime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ|1=Takiribime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932870}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ}}, and {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神|1=Ukanomitama|2=ja|3=倉稲魂神|4=de|5=Ukanomitama|6=eo|7=Ukanomitama|8=mni|9=ꯎꯀꯅꯣꯃꯤꯇꯃ|10=ru|11=Ука-но_митама|12=simple|13=Ukanomitama|14=sv|15=Ukanomitama|16=zh|17=宇迦之御魂神|lt=Ukanomitama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku}} (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|en|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|WD=Q3090616|lt=Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu}} enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|1=Minamoto no Morofusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564069}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher Ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin’i|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神階|1=shinkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591025}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajuji family|1=Kazan'in family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615289}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}}, the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}} and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * {{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan}} 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * {{ill|Kenji (era)|en|Kenji (era)|WD=Q1327993|lt=Kenchō}} 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}} style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushinadahime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|en|Gion Matsuri|WD=Q979873|lt=Gion Matsuri}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} and Emperor {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|en|Emperor Sutoku|WD=Q349287|lt=Sutoku}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} * {{Wayback|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=1701|title=Takiiribime Shrine|date=20041121152814}} * [https://ja.kyoto.travel/tourism/single02.php?Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category_id=7&tourism_id=666 Munakata Shrine - Kyoto Travel Navi] {{Munakata Faith}} {{translated page|ja|宗像神社 (京都市)|version=94851838|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:宗像神社 (京都市)]] {{Commons category|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} [[zh:宗像神社 (京都市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11451877}} [[Category:Buildings in Kamigyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Kyoto]] [[Category:Fu-sha]] [[Category:Heian-kyō]] [[Category:History of Kamigyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Kazan'in family]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ}}
{{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shrine rank|WD=Q10444029}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shrine rank|WD=Q10444029}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiiribime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ|1=Takiribime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932870}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ}}, and {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神|1=Ukanomitama|2=ja|3=倉稲魂神|4=de|5=Ukanomitama|6=eo|7=Ukanomitama|8=mni|9=ꯎꯀꯅꯣꯃꯤꯇꯃ|10=ru|11=Ука-но_митама|12=simple|13=Ukanomitama|14=sv|15=Ukanomitama|16=zh|17=宇迦之御魂神|lt=Ukanomitama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku}} (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|en|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|WD=Q3090616|lt=Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu}} enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|1=Minamoto no Morofusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564069}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher Ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin’i|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神階|1=shinkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591025}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajuji family|1=Kazan'in family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615289}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}}, the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}} and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * {{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan}} 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * {{ill|Kenji (era)|en|Kenji (era)|WD=Q1327993|lt=Kenchō}} 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}} style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushinadahime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|en|Gion Matsuri|WD=Q979873|lt=Gion Matsuri}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} and Emperor {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|en|Emperor Sutoku|WD=Q349287|lt=Sutoku}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} * {{Wayback|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=1701|title=Takiiribime Shrine|date=20041121152814}} * [https://ja.kyoto.travel/tourism/single02.php?Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category_id=7&tourism_id=666 Munakata Shrine - Kyoto Travel Navi] {{Munakata Faith}} {{translated page|ja|宗像神社 (京都市)|version=94851838|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:宗像神社 (京都市)]] {{Commons category|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} [[zh:宗像神社 (京都市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11451877}} [[Category:Buildings in Kamigyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Kyoto]] [[Category:Fu-sha]] [[Category:Heian-kyō]] [[Category:History of Kamigyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Kazan'in family]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Worship Hall |location = Kyoto City, Kamigyo Ward, within Kyoto Gyoen 9 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|08.4|N|135|45|40.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiiribime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ|1=Takiribime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932870}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ}}
{{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shrine rank|WD=Q10444029}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shrine rank|WD=Q10444029}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiiribime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ|1=Takiribime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932870}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ}}, and {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神|1=Ukanomitama|2=ja|3=倉稲魂神|4=de|5=Ukanomitama|6=eo|7=Ukanomitama|8=mni|9=ꯎꯀꯅꯣꯃꯤꯇꯃ|10=ru|11=Ука-но_митама|12=simple|13=Ukanomitama|14=sv|15=Ukanomitama|16=zh|17=宇迦之御魂神|lt=Ukanomitama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku}} (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|en|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|WD=Q3090616|lt=Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu}} enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|1=Minamoto no Morofusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564069}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher Ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin’i|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神階|1=shinkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591025}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajuji family|1=Kazan'in family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615289}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}}, the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}} and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * {{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan}} 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * {{ill|Kenji (era)|en|Kenji (era)|WD=Q1327993|lt=Kenchō}} 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}} style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushinadahime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|en|Gion Matsuri|WD=Q979873|lt=Gion Matsuri}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} and Emperor {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|en|Emperor Sutoku|WD=Q349287|lt=Sutoku}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} * {{Wayback|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=1701|title=Takiiribime Shrine|date=20041121152814}} * [https://ja.kyoto.travel/tourism/single02.php?Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category_id=7&tourism_id=666 Munakata Shrine - Kyoto Travel Navi] {{Munakata Faith}} {{translated page|ja|宗像神社 (京都市)|version=94851838|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:宗像神社 (京都市)]] {{Commons category|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} [[zh:宗像神社 (京都市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11451877}} [[Category:Buildings in Kamigyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Kyoto]] [[Category:Fu-sha]] [[Category:Heian-kyō]] [[Category:History of Kamigyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Kazan'in family]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)]][[ja:宗像神社 (京都市)]][[zh:宗像神社 (京都市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11451877}}

[[co:commons:Category:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)]][[ja:宗像神社 (京都市)]][[zh:宗像神社 (京都市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11451877}} [[Category:Shikigesha\]] [[Category:Shikigesha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Shinto shrine |Name = Munakata Shrine |Image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), haisho.jpg|280px]]
Worship hall |Location = 9, Kyoto Gyoen, Kamigyō Ward, Kyoto City |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|08.4|N|135|45|40.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = Tagorihime-no-Mikoto
Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto
Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank = Listed shrine in national histories / Former prefectural shrine |Founded = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795) |Main hall = One-bay nagare-zukuri, cypress bark roofing |Alternative names = |Pilgrimage sites = |Annual festival = [[September 15]] |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (Munakata-jinja) is a Shinto shrine located within the Kyoto Imperial Garden in Kamigyō Ward, Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture. It is a shrine listed in the national histories, and its former shrine rank was prefectural shrine. == Enshrined deities == The shrine principally enshrines the '''Three Munakata Goddesses''' (Tagorihime-no-Mikoto, Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto, and Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto), with Inari-no-Kami (Ukanomitama-no-Kami) and '''Ama-no-Iwato-Ake-no-Kami''' additionally enshrined. The principal deities are recorded in the ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' as being the “same deities,” although enshrined at a different location, as the Munakata Shrine of Chikuzen Province (present-day Munakata Taisha), and are also described as “the same deities at a separate shrine.” The subsidiary deity Ama-no-Iwato-Ake-no-Kami was likewise incorporated from a shrine listed in the national histories (Ame-no-Ishido-Ake-no-Kami). The ''Kazan’in Family Chronicle'' (quoted in the ''Yamashiro Meishōshi'') records its state before enshrinement as: “Ame-no-Ishido-Ake-no-Kami. A great stone. Possessing spiritual power.” Ukanomitama-no-Kami is said to have been enshrined together by Fujiwara no Tokihira. As the shrine is currently located in the southwestern part of the Imperial Garden (the “rear demon gate”), it has attracted faith as a protector against misfortune. == History == According to shrine tradition, in Enryaku 14 (795), Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu, acting on an imperial command from Emperor Kanmu, invited the Munakata deity of Chikuzen as a guardian deity of the imperial residence and enshrined it at the southwestern corner of his own residence, the Tōkyō Residence (also called the Tōkyō Ichijō Residence). As the oldest source recording the origin of the shrine’s location, the ''Tsuchiyuki'' by Minamoto no Morofusa is cited. After Fuyutsugu’s death, the Tōkyō Residence was divided into the eastern Kazan’in and the western Koichijō Residence. The same work records the following account told by the then master of the Koichijō Residence, Fujiwara no Morishige: The Koichijō Residence had been purchased by Fujiwara no Uchinaro for his son Fuyutsugu because, when Fuyutsugu was still an Inner Palace Guard, the Munakata deity called out to him from the void while he was on his way to court, delivering an oracle that if he asked his father to buy the Koichijō Residence, lived there, and enshrined the Munakata deity beside him, the deity would protect his descendants for generations. It appears that Ama-no-Ishido-Ake-no-Kami was also enshrined at the northeastern corner of the residence, though its origin is unknown. The Tōkyō Residence was the birthplace of Emperor Seiwa in Kashō 3 (850) (his mother was Akiko, a granddaughter of Fuyutsugu), and for this reason the shrine came to be revered as his birth deity and tutelary deity. The three Munakata deities were granted the rank of Senior Second Rank in Jōgan 1 (859), later promoted to Junior First Rank, and in Jōgan 7 (865) Ama-no-Ishido-Ake-no-Kami, also a residence shrine, was granted Junior Third Rank. In the same year, shields, halberds, and saddles were especially dedicated to the shrine, though it was not listed in the ''Engishiki Shrine Register''. Later, during the time of Fujiwara no Tokihira, Ukanomitama-no-Kami was enshrined together. In Tenshō 1 (1131), when Fujiwara no Iezane inherited the Kazan’in (by then known as Kazan’in after having served as the imperial residence of Emperor Kazan) from Fujiwara no Morozane and established the Kazan’in family, Ama-no-Ishido-Ake-no-Kami was also enshrined together as the family’s guardian deity. The shrine was first listed as an official shrine in Kenji 1 (1275). The shrine was destroyed by fire in the warfare of the Ōnin War, but was later rebuilt. As the surrounding area developed into a district of court nobles’ residences, the shrine remained at its location even after the Kazan’in family relocated. With Emperor Meiji’s transfer of the capital to Tokyo, the shrine came to be located within the Imperial Garden, and in Meiji 8 (1875) it was designated a prefectural shrine. After World War II, it became affiliated with the Association of Shinto Shrines. === Divine ranks === The three Munakata deities are recorded as having been granted Junior Second Rank with Eighth Order of Merit on the 30th day of the second month of Jōgan 1, promoted to Senior Second Rank the same day, and further promoted to Junior First Rank on October 11 of Jōgan 6 (864), according to the ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku''. In Kenji 1, the shrine was designated to receive offerings at the four annual imperial rites (the Kinensai, Tsukinamisai, and Niinamesai). The ''Ōkagami'' and ''Teiō Hennenk i'' also transmit a tale that Fujiwara no Yoshifusa, son of Fuyutsugu, was in constant conversation with the Munakata deity, and that, feeling apologetic that the deity’s rank was lower than his own, he arranged for the divine rank to be raised. Ama-no-Ishido-Ake-no-Kami was promoted from no rank to Junior Third Rank on March 21 of Jōgan 7. == Ritual administration == From the time the Kazan’in family inherited the estate until the Meiji period, members of that family served as bettō (administrators) and conducted the shrine’s rites. == Shrine buildings == The main hall is a one-bay nagare-zukuri structure with cypress bark roofing. It was rebuilt in the Ansei era of the Edo period (mid-19th century). == Sub-shrines == * **Hanei Inari Shrine** – Enshrines the Myōbu Inari deity. The ''Kazan’in Family Chronicle'' records that Fujiwara no Mototsune, adoptive son of Yoshifusa, enshrined it as his guardian deity. The ''Tsuchiyuki'' relates that when Mototsune was still of low rank, he rescued a fox being beaten by children; the fox later appeared in a dream and vowed to ward off calamities if given a place to dwell, and thus was assigned the present site as an attendant of the Munakata deity. One-bay nagare-zukuri with cypress bark roofing. * **Shōshōi Shrine** – Enshrines Kushinadahime-no-Kami. It is said to have been relocated from the former temporary shrine of Yasaka Shrine between the neighborhoods of Shōshōi and Shōshōi Otabi. On July 24, during the Gion Festival’s later procession, priests from Yasaka Shrine still visit to offer sacred food and report the festival’s observance. Structure: nagare-mise-dana-zukuri with cypress bark roofing. * **Kotohira Shrine** – Enshrines Ōmononushi-no-Kami and Emperor Sutoku. It was founded on October 10, Bunka 3 (1806), when Kyōgoku Takahiro, lord of the Marugame Domain in Sanuki Province, invited the deity of Kotohira Shrine. Structure: Kasuga-zukuri with cypress bark roofing. * **Kazan Inari Shrine** – Located beneath a great camphor tree south of the precinct, enshrining Ukanomitama-no-Kami. It is said to have been revered as the guardian deity of the Kazan’in family. One-bay nagare-zukuri with cypress bark roofing. The camphor tree is said to be about 600 years old (as of 2010) and the oldest in the Imperial Garden. * **Kyoto Tourism Shrine** – Enshrines Sarutahiko Ōkami. Founded on November 1, Shōwa 44 (1969) at the initiative of tourism operators to pray for visitors’ safety and the prosperity of the industry, inviting Sarutahiko as a guide deity. One-bay nagare-zukuri with copper plate roofing. == Precincts == * **Former Sakon no Sakura** – Transplanted in Shōwa 11 (1936) when the Left Cherry Tree before the Shishinden of the Kyoto Imperial Palace was replanted. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Izumoji Yoshikazu, ''Historical Cultural Properties and Personal Sites Centered on the Kamigyō Area'', Kyoto Kita Rotary Club, 1978 * Edited by Naokazu Miyaji and Ariyoshi Saeki, ''Shintō Encyclopedia'' (facsimile abridged edition), Rinsen Shoten, 1969 * ''Place Names of Kyoto City'' (Japanese Historical Place Names Series, Vol. 27), Heibonsha, 1979 == External links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} * Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection” ** Wayback links * Munakata Shrine (Kyoto City Tourism Association “Kyoto Travel Navi”) {{DEFAULTSORT:Munakata Shrine Kyoto City}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto City]] [[Category:History of Kamigyō Ward]] [[Category:Shrines listed in national histories]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Heian-kyō]] [[Category:Munakata shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kamigyō Ward]] [[Category:Kyoto Imperial Garden]] [[Category:Kazan’in family]]

Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto

It was rebuilt in the Ansei era of the Edo period (mid-19th century). == Sub-shrines == * **Hanei Inari Shrine** – Enshrines the Myōbu Inari deity. The ''Kazan’in Family Chronicle'' records that Fujiwara no Mototsune, adoptive son of Yoshifusa, enshrined it as his guardian deity. The ''Tsuchiyuki'' relates that when Mototsune was still of low rank, he rescued a fox being beaten by children; the fox later appeared in a dream and vowed to ward off calamities if given a place to dwell, and thus was assigned the present site as an attendant of the Munakata deity. One-bay nagare-zukuri with cypress bark roofing. * **Shōshōi Shrine** – Enshrines Kushinadahime-no-Kami. It is said to have been relocated from the former temporary shrine of Yasaka Shrine between the neighborhoods of Shōshōi and Shōshōi Otabi. On July 24, during the Gion Festival’s later procession, priests from Yasaka Shrine still visit to offer sacred food and report the festival’s observance. Structure: nagare-mise-dana-zukuri with cypress bark roofing. * **Kotohira Shrine** – Enshrines Ōmononushi-no-Kami and Emperor Sutoku. It was founded on October 10, Bunka 3 (1806), when Kyōgoku Takahiro, lord of the Marugame Domain in Sanuki Province, invited the deity of Kotohira Shrine. Structure: Kasuga-zukuri with cypress bark roofing. * **Kazan Inari Shrine** – Located beneath a great camphor tree south of the precinct, enshrining Ukanomitama-no-Kami. It is said to have been revered as the guardian deity of the Kazan’in family. One-bay nagare-zukuri with cypress bark roofing. The camphor tree is said to be about 600 years old (as of 2010) and the oldest in the Imperial Garden. * **Kyoto Tourism Shrine** – Enshrines Sarutahiko Ōkami. Founded on November 1, Shōwa 44 (1969) at the initiative of tourism operators to pray for visitors’ safety and the prosperity of the industry, inviting Sarutahiko as a guide deity. One-bay nagare-zukuri with copper plate roofing. == Precincts == * **Former Sakon no Sakura** – Transplanted in Shōwa 11 (1936) when the Left Cherry Tree before the Shishinden of the Kyoto Imperial Palace was replanted. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Izumoji Yoshikazu, ''Historical Cultural Properties and Personal Sites Centered on the Kamigyō Area'', Kyoto Kita Rotary Club, 1978 * Edited by Naokazu Miyaji and Ariyoshi Saeki, ''Shintō Encyclopedia'' (facsimile abridged edition), Rinsen Shoten, 1969 * ''Place Names of Kyoto City'' (Japanese Historical Place Names Series, Vol. 27), Heibonsha, 1979 == External links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} * Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection” ** Wayback links * Munakata Shrine (Kyoto City Tourism Association “Kyoto Travel Navi”) {{DEFAULTSORT:Munakata Shrine Kyoto City}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto City]] [[Category:History of Kamigyō Ward]] [[Category:Shrines listed in national histories]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Heian-kyō]] [[Category:Munakata shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kamigyō Ward]] [[Category:Kyoto Imperial Garden]] [[Category:Kazan’in family]] [[Category:pages]]

[[co:commons:Category:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)]][[ja:宗像神社 (京都市)]][[zh:宗像神社 (京都市)]] [[co:commons:Category:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)]][[ja:宗像神社 (京都市)]][[zh:宗像神社 (京都市)]] [[sn:commons:Category:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)]][[ja:宗像神社 (京都市)]][[zh:宗像神社 (京都市)]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), haisho.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = Kyoto City, Kamigyo Ward, within Kyoto Gyoen 9 |coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|08.4|N|135|45|40.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiiribime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ|1=Takiribime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932870}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ}}
{{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}} |Rank = {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shrine rank|WD=Q10444029}}, Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |Establishment = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795 CE) |Main Hall = One-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}} with Hinoki bark roofing |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = September 15 |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located within the Kyoto Gyoen in Kamigyo Ward, Kyoto City. It is a {{ill|Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja|国史見在社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Genzai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shrine rank|WD=Q10444029}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. == Enshrined Deities == Munakata Shrine enshrines the {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}: {{ill|Takiiribime|ja|多紀理比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiiribime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ|1=Takiribime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10932870}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|多岐都比売命|1=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タギツヒメ}}, and {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市寸島比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}, as its main deities. Additionally, the shrine includes {{ill|Kuraokami|ja|倉稲魂神|1=Ukanomitama|2=ja|3=倉稲魂神|4=de|5=Ukanomitama|6=eo|7=Ukanomitama|8=mni|9=ꯎꯀꯅꯣꯃꯤꯇꯃ|10=ru|11=Ука-но_митама|12=simple|13=Ukanomitama|14=sv|15=Ukanomitama|16=zh|17=宇迦之御魂神|lt=Ukanomitama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}} and {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as auxiliary deities. The shrine is believed to have been dedicated to the Munakata Goddesses under the order of Emperor Kanmu to protect the imperial court. {{ill|Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja|天岩戸開神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Iwato-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was later merged into the shrine and believed to have been revered for its connection to the imperial family and other historical events. == History == === Establishment === According to tradition, Munakata Shrine was established in the 14th year of {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku}} (795 CE) under the orders of Emperor Kanmu. {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|en|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|WD=Q3090616|lt=Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu}} enshrined the goddesses on the southwestern corner of his residence, known as the "Tokyo Residence" (東京第), as a protective measure for the imperial court. Historical records, including the ''土右記'' by {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源師房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|1=Minamoto no Morofusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564069}}, suggest that the site was chosen based on divine revelations received by Fuyutsugu while he served the imperial household. === Later Developments === The shrine gained prominence as the "birthplace" of Emperor Seiwa in 850 CE, which led to the Munakata Goddesses being granted higher Ranks, eventually reaching {{ill|Shin’i|ja|神位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin’i|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神階|1=shinkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591025}} of Junior First Rank in 864 CE. Over time, the shrine came under the patronage of the {{ill|Kajuji family|ja|花山院家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajuji family|1=Kazan'in family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615289}}, who incorporated additional deities into the site. Despite suffering damage during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}}, the shrine was rebuilt and continued to be a significant site of worship. It was designated as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}} and remains a place of reverence within Kyoto Gyoen. === Divine Rankings === * {{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan}} 1 (859 CE): Elevated to Senior Second Rank. * Jōgan 6 (864 CE): Elevated to Junior First Rank. * {{ill|Kenji (era)|en|Kenji (era)|WD=Q1327993|lt=Kenchō}} 1 (1275 CE): Included in imperial offerings for major festivals. == Festivals and Rituals == The shrine's main annual festival is held on September 15. It includes traditional rituals honoring the shrine's deities for their protection and blessings. == Shrine Architecture == The main hall is constructed in the one-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}} style with Hinoki bark roofing. The current structure dates to the late {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, having been rebuilt after previous destruction. == Sub-Shrines == * Prosperity Inari Shrine: Dedicated to Myōbu Inari. According to lore, the shrine was established after a fox appeared in a dream of Fujiwara no Mototsune. * Shōshōi Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Kushinadahime|ja|櫛稲田姫神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushinadahime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クシナダヒメ}}. It serves as a temporary resting place for deities during the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|en|Gion Matsuri|WD=Q979873|lt=Gion Matsuri}}. * Konpira Shrine: Dedicated to {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} and Emperor {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|en|Emperor Sutoku|WD=Q349287|lt=Sutoku}}. * Hanayama Inari Shrine: A shrine under a 600-year-old camphor tree within the grounds. == Access == The shrine is located within Kyoto Gyoen and can be reached via public transportation from Kyoto City. It is a 10-minute walk from the nearest bus stop. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Keiwa Izumoji, ''Historical and Cultural Sites of Kamigyo,'' Kyoto North Rotary Club, 1978. * Miyachi Naoichi and Saeki Ariyoshi, ''Shinto Encyclopedia,'' Rin-sensha, 1969 (Revised Edition). * ''The Topography of Kyoto,'' Heibonsha, 1979. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} * {{Wayback|url=http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=1701|title=Takiiribime Shrine|date=20041121152814}} * [https://ja.kyoto.travel/tourism/single02.php?Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category_id=7&tourism_id=666 Munakata Shrine - Kyoto Travel Navi] {{Munakata Faith}} {{translated page|ja|宗像神社 (京都市)|version=94851838|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:宗像神社 (京都市)]] {{Commons category|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} [[zh:宗像神社 (京都市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11451877}} [[Category:Buildings in Kamigyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Kyoto]] [[Category:Fu-sha]] [[Category:Heian-kyō]] [[Category:History of Kamigyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Kazan'in family]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

[[co:commons:Category:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)]][[ja:宗像神社 (京都市)]][[zh:宗像神社 (京都市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11451877}} [[Category:Shikigesha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Shinto shrine |Name = Munakata Shrine |Image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), haisho.jpg|280px]]
Worship hall |Location = 9, Kyoto Gyoen, Kamigyō Ward, Kyoto City |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|01|08.4|N|135|45|40.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = Tagorihime-no-Mikoto
Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto
Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank = Listed shrine in national histories / Former prefectural shrine |Founded = Traditionally Enryaku 14 (795) |Main hall = One-bay nagare-zukuri, cypress bark roofing |Alternative names = |Pilgrimage sites = |Annual festival = [[September 15]] |Rituals = }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Kyoto Gyoen), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (Munakata-jinja) is a Shinto shrine located within the Kyoto Imperial Garden in Kamigyō Ward, Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture. It is a shrine listed in the national histories, and its former shrine rank was prefectural shrine. == Enshrined deities == The shrine principally enshrines the '''Three Munakata Goddesses''' (Tagorihime-no-Mikoto, Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto, and Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto), with Inari-no-Kami (Ukanomitama-no-Kami) and '''Ama-no-Iwato-Ake-no-Kami''' additionally enshrined. The principal deities are recorded in the ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' as being the “same deities,” although enshrined at a different location, as the Munakata Shrine of Chikuzen Province (present-day Munakata Taisha), and are also described as “the same deities at a separate shrine.” The subsidiary deity Ama-no-Iwato-Ake-no-Kami was likewise incorporated from a shrine listed in the national histories (Ame-no-Ishido-Ake-no-Kami). The ''Kazan’in Family Chronicle'' (quoted in the ''Yamashiro Meishōshi'') records its state before enshrinement as: “Ame-no-Ishido-Ake-no-Kami. A great stone. Possessing spiritual power.” Ukanomitama-no-Kami is said to have been enshrined together by Fujiwara no Tokihira. As the shrine is currently located in the southwestern part of the Imperial Garden (the “rear demon gate”), it has attracted faith as a protector against misfortune. == History == According to shrine tradition, in Enryaku 14 (795), Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu, acting on an imperial command from Emperor Kanmu, invited the Munakata deity of Chikuzen as a guardian deity of the imperial residence and enshrined it at the southwestern corner of his own residence, the Tōkyō Residence (also called the Tōkyō Ichijō Residence). As the oldest source recording the origin of the shrine’s location, the ''Tsuchiyuki'' by Minamoto no Morofusa is cited. After Fuyutsugu’s death, the Tōkyō Residence was divided into the eastern Kazan’in and the western Koichijō Residence. The same work records the following account told by the then master of the Koichijō Residence, Fujiwara no Morishige: The Koichijō Residence had been purchased by Fujiwara no Uchinaro for his son Fuyutsugu because, when Fuyutsugu was still an Inner Palace Guard, the Munakata deity called out to him from the void while he was on his way to court, delivering an oracle that if he asked his father to buy the Koichijō Residence, lived there, and enshrined the Munakata deity beside him, the deity would protect his descendants for generations. It appears that Ama-no-Ishido-Ake-no-Kami was also enshrined at the northeastern corner of the residence, though its origin is unknown. The Tōkyō Residence was the birthplace of Emperor Seiwa in Kashō 3 (850) (his mother was Akiko, a granddaughter of Fuyutsugu), and for this reason the shrine came to be revered as his birth deity and tutelary deity. The three Munakata deities were granted the rank of Senior Second Rank in Jōgan 1 (859), later promoted to Junior First Rank, and in Jōgan 7 (865) Ama-no-Ishido-Ake-no-Kami, also a residence shrine, was granted Junior Third Rank. In the same year, shields, halberds, and saddles were especially dedicated to the shrine, though it was not listed in the ''Engishiki Shrine Register''. Later, during the time of Fujiwara no Tokihira, Ukanomitama-no-Kami was enshrined together. In Tenshō 1 (1131), when Fujiwara no Iezane inherited the Kazan’in (by then known as Kazan’in after having served as the imperial residence of Emperor Kazan) from Fujiwara no Morozane and established the Kazan’in family, Ama-no-Ishido-Ake-no-Kami was also enshrined together as the family’s guardian deity. The shrine was first listed as an official shrine in Kenji 1 (1275). The shrine was destroyed by fire in the warfare of the Ōnin War, but was later rebuilt. As the surrounding area developed into a district of court nobles’ residences, the shrine remained at its location even after the Kazan’in family relocated. With Emperor Meiji’s transfer of the capital to Tokyo, the shrine came to be located within the Imperial Garden, and in Meiji 8 (1875) it was designated a prefectural shrine. After World War II, it became affiliated with the Association of Shinto Shrines. === Divine ranks === The three Munakata deities are recorded as having been granted Junior Second Rank with Eighth Order of Merit on the 30th day of the second month of Jōgan 1, promoted to Senior Second Rank the same day, and further promoted to Junior First Rank on October 11 of Jōgan 6 (864), according to the ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku''. In Kenji 1, the shrine was designated to receive offerings at the four annual imperial rites (the Kinensai, Tsukinamisai, and Niinamesai). The ''Ōkagami'' and ''Teiō Hennenk i'' also transmit a tale that Fujiwara no Yoshifusa, son of Fuyutsugu, was in constant conversation with the Munakata deity, and that, feeling apologetic that the deity’s rank was lower than his own, he arranged for the divine rank to be raised. Ama-no-Ishido-Ake-no-Kami was promoted from no rank to Junior Third Rank on March 21 of Jōgan 7. == Ritual administration == From the time the Kazan’in family inherited the estate until the Meiji period, members of that family served as bettō (administrators) and conducted the shrine’s rites. == Shrine buildings == The main hall is a one-bay nagare-zukuri structure with cypress bark roofing. It was rebuilt in the Ansei era of the Edo period (mid-19th century). == Sub-shrines == * **Hanei Inari Shrine** – Enshrines the Myōbu Inari deity. The ''Kazan’in Family Chronicle'' records that Fujiwara no Mototsune, adoptive son of Yoshifusa, enshrined it as his guardian deity. The ''Tsuchiyuki'' relates that when Mototsune was still of low rank, he rescued a fox being beaten by children; the fox later appeared in a dream and vowed to ward off calamities if given a place to dwell, and thus was assigned the present site as an attendant of the Munakata deity. One-bay nagare-zukuri with cypress bark roofing. * **Shōshōi Shrine** – Enshrines Kushinadahime-no-Kami. It is said to have been relocated from the former temporary shrine of Yasaka Shrine between the neighborhoods of Shōshōi and Shōshōi Otabi. On July 24, during the Gion Festival’s later procession, priests from Yasaka Shrine still visit to offer sacred food and report the festival’s observance. Structure: nagare-mise-dana-zukuri with cypress bark roofing. * **Kotohira Shrine** – Enshrines Ōmononushi-no-Kami and Emperor Sutoku. It was founded on October 10, Bunka 3 (1806), when Kyōgoku Takahiro, lord of the Marugame Domain in Sanuki Province, invited the deity of Kotohira Shrine. Structure: Kasuga-zukuri with cypress bark roofing. * **Kazan Inari Shrine** – Located beneath a great camphor tree south of the precinct, enshrining Ukanomitama-no-Kami. It is said to have been revered as the guardian deity of the Kazan’in family. One-bay nagare-zukuri with cypress bark roofing. The camphor tree is said to be about 600 years old (as of 2010) and the oldest in the Imperial Garden. * **Kyoto Tourism Shrine** – Enshrines Sarutahiko Ōkami. Founded on November 1, Shōwa 44 (1969) at the initiative of tourism operators to pray for visitors’ safety and the prosperity of the industry, inviting Sarutahiko as a guide deity. One-bay nagare-zukuri with copper plate roofing. == Precincts == * **Former Sakon no Sakura** – Transplanted in Shōwa 11 (1936) when the Left Cherry Tree before the Shishinden of the Kyoto Imperial Palace was replanted. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Izumoji Yoshikazu, ''Historical Cultural Properties and Personal Sites Centered on the Kamigyō Area'', Kyoto Kita Rotary Club, 1978 * Edited by Naokazu Miyaji and Ariyoshi Saeki, ''Shintō Encyclopedia'' (facsimile abridged edition), Rinsen Shoten, 1969 * ''Place Names of Kyoto City'' (Japanese Historical Place Names Series, Vol. 27), Heibonsha, 1979 == External links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Kyoto)}} * Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto and Shrine Historical Sources Collection” ** Wayback links * Munakata Shrine (Kyoto City Tourism Association “Kyoto Travel Navi”) {{DEFAULTSORT:Munakata Shrine Kyoto City}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto City]] [[Category:History of Kamigyō Ward]] [[Category:Shrines listed in national histories]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Heian-kyō]] [[Category:Munakata shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kamigyō Ward]] [[Category:Kyoto Imperial Garden]] [[Category:Kazan’in family]] [[Category:pages]]
